mnemonics: Higher Powers, Higher Steps: To find the probability of moving from state i to state j in exactly n steps, raise the one-step transition matrix P to its nth power (Pⁿ). The (i, j) entry of this resulting matrix is your answer.
keyPoints: The n-step transition probability is denoted as pᵢⱼ⁽ⁿ⁾, the probability of being in state j after n steps, starting from state i.
The Chapman-Kolmogorov equations provide a way to compute these probabilities by considering intermediate states: pᵢⱼ⁽ⁿ⁾⁺ᵐ⁾ = ∑ₖ pᵢₖ⁽ⁿ⁾ pₖⱼ⁽ᵐ⁾.
These probabilities are found by computing the nth power of the one-step transition probability matrix P. Specifically, pᵢⱼ⁽ⁿ⁾ is the (i, j)-th entry of the matrix Pⁿ.
commonMistakes: Confusing Pⁿ (matrix raised to power n) with n*P (scalar multiplication). Pⁿ represents sequential transitions, not amplified probabilities.
Misindexing the states when reading the (i, j) entry from the computed Pⁿ matrix. Remember: row i is the starting state, column j is the ending state.
Assuming the chain must have returned to a state by a certain step. The probabilities are for exactly n steps, and the path is not guaranteed.
